cases.scope: Review the just-written files in fixtures/duration-seconds-helper/: a new Duration+Seconds.swift extension and the MockDetector code that uses it.

expected_findings.claim: The `inSeconds` interface (Duration.inSeconds) need not exist. Read its call sites in MockDetector (timingSigma, onBeatHit, freeHit): every use converts a Duration to Double seconds and immediately wraps the result back into a Duration via `.seconds(...)`. The callers only ever do Duration-to-Duration arithmetic (scale by a factor, add, clamp), so the intermediate Double-seconds representation the interface exposes is unnecessary; the extension should be removed and the call sites should operate on Duration directly.

forbidden_findings: Do not approve the helper as fine just because `inSeconds` reads as a clean one-line property in isolation — its redundancy is only visible from how the call sites use it.
Do not reduce it to a file-placement / separation-of-concerns nit (e.g. 'move the extension elsewhere') without questioning whether the interface should exist at all.
cases.grading: PASS when target_reviewer, by reading the call sites, raises that the `inSeconds` interface need not exist — its callers round-trip Duration -> Double -> Duration and only need Duration arithmetic, so the extension should be removed — at confidence >= expected_min_confidence, AND the report contains none of forbidden_findings. Approving the helper, reporting no structure issue, or only nitpicking its file placement without questioning its existence, is FAIL.

origin.note: review-board fanned out to 5 reviewers; all missed this. reuse-reviewer was correct in its own lens (the stdlib has no Double-seconds accessor); the miss is structural — the interface is unnecessary given its call sites — which is why this case targets structure-reviewer. The human questioned it (L142), which led to deleting the file (L164) and rewriting 3 call sites to use Duration operators directly (L171-177). This case is the reconstructed false negative; intent is withheld so the reviewer must derive the interface's necessity from usage.
